turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE ROBERTS
turns.text: Justice Sotomayor has the opinion of the Court in Case 22-660, Murray versus UBS Securities.

turns.name: JUSTICE SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: Section 1514A of the Sarbanes-Oxley Act protects whistleblowers who report corporate fraud or securities laws violations. It prohibits any publicly traded company from taking an adverse employment action against a whistleblower because of his protective activity. Petitioner Trevor Murray brought suit under this provision in Federal Court, alleging that Respondent, UBS Securities, fired him from his job as a research strategist because of his protected activity. As Petitioner explained to the Jury, the firing came shortly after Petitioner had reported to his supervisor that leaders of the UBS Trading Desk were pressuring him to skew his research reports to be more supportive of their business goals. Petitioner had a duty to certify to the SEC that his reports were independently produced and reflected his own views, and he believed that the efforts to skew his reports were both unethical and illegal. The Jury ruled for Petitioner finding that he had shown that his protected activity was a contributing factor in his firing as the Statute required. The Jury also found that Respondent had failed to prove that it would have fired Petitioner even if he had not engaged in the protective activity, rejecting Respondent’s argument, that market-wide difficulties had forced the company to eliminate Petitioner’s role. The Jury awarded Petitioner nearly $1 million. But the Second Circuit vacated the Jury’s verdict. It held that the District Court should have instructed the Jury that Petitioner was required to prove not only that his protective activity was a contributing factor in the firing, but that Respondent acted with retaliatory intent. The question here is whether the Second Circuit was correct to require Section 1514A plaintiffs to prove their employer’s retaliatory intent because it was not, this Court now reverses. Section 1514A provides that employers may not discharge, demote, suspend, threaten, harass, or in any other manner, discriminate against an employee in the terms and conditions of employment because of protected whistleblowing activity. Respondent argues that this case turns on the word discriminate, which it says imports a requirement of retaliatory intent akin to animus, because the word discriminate cannot bear the weight that Respondent places on it. This Court has said that the normal definition of discrimination is differential treatment. When an employer treats a whistleblower differently, and worse, because of his protected whistleblowing activity, that is actionable discrimination under Section 1514A. The Statute’s mandatory burden-shifting framework further confirms that a plaintiff need not prove retaliatory intent. While Congress could have required whistleblowers to make this heightened showing, it instead required only a showing that the protected activity was a contributing factor in the adverse employment action. When a whistleblower makes that showing, the burden shifts to the employer to show that it would have taken the same unfavorable personnel action in the absence of the protected activity. That makes sense. Employers typically hold all the cards in employment discrimination cases, and burden shifting forces them to come forth with a response to the employee’s circumstantial evidence of intent. Requiring a plaintiff to provide definitive proof of an employer’s retaliatory intent would displace this framework and ignore Congress’ choice. For these and other reasons set forth in the opinion, the Court reverses and remands for further proceedings consistent with its opinion. Justice Alito has filed a concurring opinion, which Justice Barrett has joined.
